title: Northbridge Learning Studio claim audit
educational_scenario: A fully fictional teaching bundle about low-stakes study workshops. Learners compare bounded source summaries with claims of different strength.

source_provenance.authoring_method: Human-authored from scratch for the ScholarTrace Build Week demonstration.
source_provenance.license_reuse_note: Independently written downstream demonstration content for this repository.

sources.text: In the fictional Northbridge Learning Studio, 24 volunteer first-year learners attended a six-week planning workshop. Attendance records show that 20 learners completed at least five sessions.
All 18 learners in the fictional reflection group submitted at least four weekly reflection cards. The exercise did not measure confidence.
The fictional comparison summary found no difference in puzzle accuracy between learners who used colored reminder cards and learners who used plain reminder cards.

Among 40 fictional learners who chose their own study routines, checklist use was associated with higher practice-quiz scores. The groups were self-selected, and the summary states that no causal conclusion can be drawn.
A fictional four-week pilot involved 12 volunteer learners in one introductory design class. Nine reported that a two-minute planning prompt was easy to use. No later follow-up was conducted.
The fictional activity log mentions a follow-up session but supplies no attendance count, outcome measure, comparison, or duration.
One fictional session note says seven of twelve learners preferred the paper planning card.
A second fictional session note for the same group says five of twelve learners preferred the paper planning card. The records do not explain the discrepancy.

claims.text: In the six-week fictional workshop, 20 of 24 volunteer first-year learners completed at least five sessions.
The 18 fictional learners submitted reflection cards and became more confident.
Colored reminder cards improved fictional learners' puzzle accuracy.
Using a checklist caused the fictional learners to earn higher practice-quiz scores.
Two-minute planning prompts permanently help all university learners.
The fictional follow-up session sustained the earlier workshop outcome.
A majority of the fictional group preferred the paper planning card.
